meta.purpose: Define the chain of reasoning behind code review principles, moving from observation to actionable conclusion.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user asks why a code review principle exists, or requests rationale behind review practices.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why code review must be collaborative, not a gatekeeping checkpoint.
logic: When reviews feel like approval/rejection judgments, authors become defensive rather than curious.
Defensiveness leads to hidden concerns — the author stops surfacing doubts and tradeoffs.
Hidden concerns lead to unexamined decisions and missed bug opportunities.
The team loses the primary value of review: collective intelligence applied to the change.
Therefore, review posture must be collaborative from the first comment.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Every review comment must signal partnership, not judgment. The word 'you' should appear less than 'we'. Praise good decisions explicitly.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why every review comment must carry an explicit classification.
logic: An unclassified comment is ambiguous: is this a demand or a suggestion?
The author cannot prioritize ambiguous comments and will either over-react or under-react.
Over-reaction wastes time fixing non-blocking issues as if they were blocking.
Under-reaction means blocking issues may be treated as optional.
Classification eliminates the ambiguity cost — the author's next action is always clear.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Always prefix comments with [blocking], [non-blocking], [nitpick], or [question]. This single practice transforms review throughput.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why quick partial feedback is more valuable than slow complete feedback.
logic: Every hour the PR sits unreviewed, the author's context degrades and merge conflicts accumulate.
A developer context-switched away from a PR needs 15-30 minutes to reload context for follow-up changes.
Directional feedback delivered fast lets the author correct course in the same session, avoiding context loss.
Merge conflicts from delayed review create work that would not exist if the review had been faster.
Therefore, delivering one blocking observation within hours beats delivering a full line-by-line review within days.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Prioritize speed. Review the highest-risk section first and share directional feedback immediately. Follow up with depth on less critical sections.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why human review energy should not be spent on style preferences.
logic: Style preferences are encoding errors — they can be detected and fixed deterministically by tools.
Detecting a style violation requires low cognitive effort; detecting an architectural flaw requires deep domain knowledge.
When a reviewer spends attention on style, they have less cognitive capacity remaining for architecture, correctness, and security.
Style automation (formatters, linters) provides instant, fatigue-free, consistent feedback with zero reviewer attention cost.
Every team pays the same cost for manual style review; the automation cost is paid once.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Enforce style with automated tooling in CI. Reserve human review for what tools cannot do: design assessment, intent evaluation, and security reasoning.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why understanding intent must precede code evaluation.
logic: Code is a solution to a problem. Evaluating a solution without knowing the problem is guesswork.
A correct implementation of the wrong approach is a defect, not a success.
Reviewers who skip intent understanding flag 'wrong approach' in cases where the approach is deliberately constrained by requirements they don't know.
False-blocking requests (change this design that was intentionally chosen) damage reviewer credibility and author motivation.
Therefore, every review must start with intent: what problem, what constraints, what was considered and rejected.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Always read the PR description and ask intent-clarifying questions before evaluating the code itself. The first question should be 'what problem does this solve', not 'why did you code it this way'.
